aller.base_usage: Used for movement and in aller + infinitive for the near future

appeler.base_usage: Used for calling someone on the phone or naming something; reflexive s'appeler means “to be named.”

arriver.base_usage: Mainly used for reaching a place; also arriver à + infinitive = manage to

avoir.base_usage: Mainly used for possession; also an auxiliary in compound tenses and in il y a

croire.base_usage: Commonly used for beliefs; in je crois que..., it often means “I think.”

devoir.base_usage: Usually followed by an infinitive: “must” / “have to.”

dire.base_usage: Used for saying something, often with que or to tell someone something

faire.base_usage: Very common: do/make; also used causatively with an infinitive

falloir.base_usage: Impersonal only: il faut + noun, infinitive, or que + subjunctive

mettre.base_usage: General verb for putting/placing; very common in set phrases

parler.base_usage: Common for speaking, talking to someone, or speaking a language

passer.base_usage: Very common; also in fixed patterns like se passer = happen and se passer de = do without

pouvoir.translation: to can; to be able to
pouvoir.base_usage: Used with an infinitive to show ability or possibility

prendre.base_usage: Basic sense is to take; also used for having a drink/meal and catching transport

savoir.base_usage: Used for facts, and for knowing how to do something

trouver.base_usage: Also common in trouver que + clause: “to think/feel that.”

venir.base_usage: Mainly movement toward the speaker or another point of arrival; also in venir de + infinitive, "to have just done"

voir.base_usage: Most often used for physically seeing; also means noticing or realizing in clauses

vouloir.base_usage: Main verb for wanting something, to do something, or for someone to do something

être.base_usage: Main copula; also the auxiliary for passive and reflexive forms

aider.base_usage: Used for helping a person or helping someone do something

arrêter.base_usage: Commonly used for stopping something; s’arrêter means “to stop, come to a halt.”

attendre.base_usage: Use with a direct object for people or things; s'attendre à means 'to expect'

chercher.base_usage: Most often used for looking for a person or thing; also chercher à + infinitive = try to

connaître.base_usage: Used for knowing people, places, or things you are familiar with

alternate_glosses: to ask someone to do something
demander.base_usage: Often used with a direct object, à + person, or de + infinitive

devenir.base_usage: Used with a noun or adjective for changing into a new state, role, or identity

donner.base_usage: Mainly: give something to someone; also in patterns like donner à réfléchir = make you think

entendre.base_usage: Mainly used for hearing something; reflexive s’entendre avec means “to get along with.”

essayer.base_usage: Used for trying to do something; with an object, often “try on” or “test.”

finir.base_usage: Used for finishing something or finishing doing something (finir de + infinitive)

jouer.base_usage: Used for games, sports, instruments, and roles; often with jouer à or jouer de

laisser.base_usage: Also common as "laisser + infinitive": to let someone do something

oublier.base_usage: Used with an object, with de + infinitive, or que + clause

partir.base_usage: Common intransitive verb for leaving a place; takes être in compound tenses

penser.base_usage: Commonly used for thoughts and opinions; penser à = think about / remember to

regarder.base_usage: Used for directing your gaze at someone or something; also for watching an event or show

rendre.base_usage: Most often used for giving something back, often with à quelqu’un

rentrer.base_usage: Commonly means to go home or back inside; with an object, it can mean to bring something in

rester.base_usage: Used for staying in a place or remaining in a state

revenir.base_usage: Used for coming back to a place or person

s'appeler.base_usage: Reflexive: used for a person’s or thing’s name, as in ça s'appelle

se faire.base_usage: Reflexive; often se faire à qqn/qqch = to get used to sb/sth

sortir.base_usage: Commonly intransitive; can also mean ‘to take out’ with an object

acheter.base_usage: Use for getting something in exchange for money; s’acheter means buy for oneself

agir.base_usage: Also common in the impersonal il s’agit de: “it’s about” / “the point is”

aimer.base_usage: Used for liking things and loving people; also with an infinitive for preferences

apprendre.base_usage: Usually means “to learn”; with à quelqu’un, it can mean “to teach someone something.”

asseoir.base_usage: Often used in the reflexive form s'asseoir: to sit down

changer.base_usage: Used for changing something, or changing from one thing to another; also in se changer, “to get changed.”

choisir.base_usage: Most commonly used with a noun object: choose someone or something

commencer.base_usage: Used for starting something or starting to do something; also intransitive with events

comprendre.base_usage: Usually means to understand someone or something; also used for “include” in contexts like prices or offers

continuer.base_usage: Most often used as continuer à + infinitive: “keep doing.”

courir.base_usage: Used for running on foot; also for covering a distance on foot

décider.base_usage: Most often used with de + infinitive or de + noun

désoler.base_usage: Used when something saddens or upsets someone; often in ça me désole de + infinitive

entrer.base_usage: Usually intransitive: “to go/come in”; with an object, “to input” or “to bring in”

envoyer.base_usage: Most common for sending something to someone: envoyer qqch à qqn

garder.base_usage: Used for keeping or holding onto something; se garder de + infinitive means to avoid doing something

importer.base_usage: Commonly impersonal: il importe de + infinitive = it is important to

manger.base_usage: Use for eating food or a meal

montrer.base_usage: Used for showing something to someone; also for demonstrating that something is the case

mourir.base_usage: Main verb for dying; also in reflexive se mourir, “to be slowly dying.”

occuper.base_usage: Often means to take up space or time

ouvrir.base_usage: Used for opening something; reflexive s'ouvrir means “to open up.”

parfaire.base_usage: Formal verb: usually means to perfect or finish off something; also to make up a total

payer.base_usage: Use for giving money for something or to someone

perdre.base_usage: Used for losing something; reflexive se perdre means to get lost

porter.base_usage: Basic sense: to carry something; also very common for clothes: porter une robe = to wear a dress

recevoir.base_usage: Usually means to receive/get something; with guests, it can mean to host

rencontrer.base_usage: Used for meeting someone or coming across something

retrouver.base_usage: Used for finding someone or something again; reflexive se retrouver often means “meet up” or “end up.”

revoir.base_usage: Most common in study/work contexts; also reflexive: se revoir = to see each other again

s'aimer.base_usage: Reflexive reciprocal: used when two or more people love each other

s'amuser.base_usage: Reflexive verb: means to enjoy yourself or have fun, often with à + infinitive

s'asseoir.base_usage: Reflexive verb: used for sitting down or taking a seat

s'habiller.base_usage: Reflexive: used for putting on clothes

s'intéresser.base_usage: Reflexive: s'intéresser à + noun = be interested in something

s'occuper.base_usage: Reflexive: s'occuper de + noun means to look after someone/something or handle a task; with an infinitive, it means to see to doing something

sauver.base_usage: Most often used for rescuing someone/something or saving someone from harm

se battre.base_usage: Reflexive verb: used for physical fighting or for fighting against/for something

alternate_glosses: to be acquainted with each other
se connaître.base_usage: Reflexive: used when people are acquainted with each other

se coucher.base_usage: Reflexive: used for going to bed, usually at night

se demander.base_usage: Reflexive verb used for “wonder if/whether” or “wonder about” something

se laver.base_usage: Reflexive verb for washing your body

se lever.base_usage: Reflexive: used for getting out of bed or standing up

se marier.base_usage: Reflexive: used for people getting married; also for things that go well together

se mettre.base_usage: Very common in se mettre à + infinitive: “to start doing.”

se parler.base_usage: Reflexive reciprocal: people communicate with one another

se passer.base_usage: Used for events happening; se passer de means to do without

se plaindre.base_usage: Reflexive: se plaindre de = complain about; se plaindre à = complain to someone

se promener.base_usage: Reflexive: used for walking around, often for pleasure or exercise

se rappeler.base_usage: Reflexive verb: used for remembering a fact, person, or event

se rendre.base_usage: Reflexive verb; often used with se rendre à + place = go to/make one's way to

se reposer.base_usage: Reflexive; most often means to rest or relax, sometimes a body part

se retrouver.base_usage: Reflexive; commonly means to meet up, and can also mean to end up in a situation

se réveiller.base_usage: Reflexive: used for waking from sleep

se sentir.base_usage: Reflexive: used with an adjective to say how someone feels

se souvenir.base_usage: Reflexive; usually se souvenir de + noun, and it means “remember” or “recall.”

se taire.base_usage: Reflexive verb: used when someone stops speaking or chooses not to speak

se trouver.base_usage: Reflexive; commonly used for where something is located

se voir.base_usage: Reflexive; often means people see each other, and in "ça se voit" it means "it's obvious."

sembler.base_usage: Used with à quelqu’un to mean “seem to someone.”

sentir.base_usage: Often means “to feel” or “to sense”; with an object, it can mean “to smell.”

servir.base_usage: Also common in servir à ('be used for') and servir de ('act as')

souvenir.base_usage: Usually used reflexively: se souvenir de + noun/pronoun

suffire.base_usage: Often impersonal: il suffit de + infinitive = just need to / it is enough to

suivre.base_usage: Use for following a person, path, order, or instructions

tenir.base_usage: Used for physically holding something; tenir à means to care about or insist on

tirer.base_usage: Most common in the physical sense: to pull something

tomber.base_usage: Used for something moving downward under gravity

travailler.base_usage: Use for doing a job or carrying out work on something

tuer.base_usage: Used for killing people or animals; se tuer means to kill oneself

utiliser.base_usage: Use with a direct object for ordinary practical use

vivre.base_usage: Also used with an object meaning “to experience.”

abandonner.base_usage: Used for leaving someone or something behind, or giving up on a plan

accepter.base_usage: Use for agreeing to take something or agreeing to do something

accompagner.base_usage: Used for going with someone, often to a place or appointment

accorder.base_usage: Commonly used with à + person for granting something

accuser.base_usage: Usually accuser quelqu’un de + noun/infinitive: to accuse someone of something

admettre.base_usage: Most often used for admitting a fact, mistake, or truth

adorer.base_usage: Often means to love or really like something

affronter.base_usage: Used for facing something difficult or challenging

ajouter.base_usage: Used for adding something or adding a remark/fact

allumer.base_usage: Used for switching on lights, devices, etc.; also for lighting a fire or cigarette

amener.base_usage: Used for bringing people or animals; cf. apporter for things

amuser.base_usage: Used for making someone amused; reflexive s'amuser means to have fun / enjoy oneself

annoncer.base_usage: Used for publicly saying or stating something

annuler.base_usage: Used for canceling plans, events, or arrangements

apparaître.base_usage: Used for becoming visible or for seeming to be the case

appartenir.base_usage: Used for possession: appartient à + person = belongs to someone

apporter.base_usage: Use for bringing something to a place or person

apprécier.base_usage: Used for valuing something or finding it enjoyable

armer.base_usage: Most often used with a direct object: armer quelqu’un/quelque chose

arranger.base_usage: Use for organizing, fixing, or setting up things; aussi in ça m'arrange = it suits me

assassiner.base_usage: Used for killing a person deliberately, especially a public figure

assister.base_usage: Often with à: assister à + noun means to attend or be present at

associer.base_usage: Use for linking one thing to another: associer A à B

assurer.base_usage: Commonly used for ensuring or guaranteeing something; reflexive s'assurer que = to make sure that

attacher.base_usage: Use for fastening or attaching something to something else; s'attacher à means to become attached to

attaquer.base_usage: Also in s'attaquer à: to tackle a problem or issue

atteindre.base_usage: Used for reaching a place, goal, level, or point

atterrir.base_usage: Used for an aircraft landing at a place

attirer.base_usage: Used for drawing people, attention, or things toward something

attraper.base_usage: Most often means to catch or grab something; also used for catching an illness

autoriser.base_usage: Used for giving permission, often with autoriser quelqu’un à + infinitive

avancer.base_usage: Used for moving something forward or progressing; also means “to claim” in formal use

avouer.base_usage: Used for admitting a fact, fault, or secret

baiser.base_usage: Vulgar slang; the old sense “to kiss” is archaic

baisser.base_usage: Used for lowering something; reflexive se baisser means to bend down

battre.base_usage: Used for hitting something or defeating someone in a game, fight, or contest

blesser.base_usage: Commonly used for physical injury; reflexive se blesser means “to get injured.”

boire.base_usage: Used for taking liquids into the body

bosser.base_usage: Informal: often means to work hard or do your job

briser.base_usage: Used for breaking something; se briser = to break/shatter

brûler.base_usage: Most often means something is burning, or you burn something

cacher.base_usage: Used for hiding something, or hiding something from someone; reflexive se cacher = to hide oneself

calmer.base_usage: Used for calming someone or something; reflexive se calmer means “to calm down.”

carter.base_usage: Used for putting something into a box or a protective casing

casser.base_usage: Mainly used for breaking objects; se casser means to break / become broken

causer.base_usage: Used for causing an effect, result, or problem; also means 'to chat' informally

cesser.base_usage: Usually cesser de + infinitive: to stop doing something

chanter.base_usage: Used for producing music with the voice; also with an object in phrases like "chanter une chanson"

charger.base_usage: Mainly used for putting something onto a vehicle or into a machine

chasser.base_usage: Usually refers to hunting animals; also means to chase away

coincer.base_usage: Used for something becoming stuck, or for cornering/trapping someone

combattre.base_usage: Used for fighting or combating an enemy, problem, or disease

commander.base_usage: Commonly means to order food, goods, or services; also to command someone

commettre.base_usage: Used mainly for crimes, offences, or serious mistakes

compter.base_usage: Used for counting numbers or objects; also before an infinitive, it can mean “to intend.”

concentrer.base_usage: Often transitive or reflexive: concentrer quelque chose, or se concentrer = concentrate/focus

concerner.base_usage: Used for what something is about, or who/what it affects

conclure.base_usage: Often used as conclure que + clause: to conclude / infer that

conduire.base_usage: Commonly used for driving a vehicle; also in conduire à + infinitive, “to lead to.”

confier.base_usage: Usually confier qqch à qqn = to entrust something to someone; reflexive se confier à qqn = to confide in someone

confirmer.base_usage: Used for making something certain or official, e.g. confirming a reservation

conseiller.base_usage: Often used as conseiller quelqu’un de + infinitive: advise someone to do something

considérer.base_usage: Often used with que to mean “to believe that.”

construire.base_usage: Usually means building something physical; also used figuratively for careers or ideas

contacter.base_usage: Used for making contact with a person, company, or service

contenir.base_usage: Used for what a container, text, or group contains; also se contenir = to control oneself

contrôler.base_usage: Often used for official checking or inspection; se contrôler means to restrain oneself

convaincre.base_usage: Use with a direct object: to persuade someone

correspondre.base_usage: Usually used with à: “to correspond to,” “to match.”

coucher.translation: to put to bed; to lay down
coucher.base_usage: Non-reflexive: to put someone to bed or lay something down; se coucher is the usual form for 'to go to bed'

couler.base_usage: Most often intransitive: for liquid or a current to flow

couper.base_usage: Use for cutting something with a knife, scissors, etc

couvrir.base_usage: Used for putting something over something; also in passive forms like être couvert de

coûter.base_usage: Used for price; can also mean “costs X to someone” with à

craindre.base_usage: Used for fearing something; also with de + infinitive or que + clause

créer.base_usage: Used for bringing something new into existence

crémer.base_usage: Used for adding cream to coffee or a dish

danser.base_usage: Used for moving rhythmically to music; also in phrases like danser le tango

descendre.base_usage: Used for moving downward; also with objects and vehicles

deviner.base_usage: Used for guessing something or that something is the case

diriger.base_usage: Often used for leading or managing a person, team, or organization

discuter.base_usage: Usually followed by de: discuter de quelque chose or de + infinitive

disparaître.base_usage: Used when someone or something is no longer visible or present

douer.base_usage: Used for giving someone a quality, ability, or trait; often in formal or literary style

durer.base_usage: Used for how long something continues or remains in effect

débarrasser.base_usage: Used for clearing away something, or freeing someone/something from something, often with de

déclarer.base_usage: Often means to state formally, especially with que

découvrir.base_usage: Also used for finding out something, or revealing something to someone

défendre.base_usage: Mainly used for defending or protecting; with à quelqu’un, it can mean “to forbid.”

déjeuner.base_usage: Used for the midday meal in standard French

dépasser.base_usage: Used for going beyond a limit or standard; se dépasser means to outdo oneself

dépendre.base_usage: Usually takes de: dépendre de qqn/qqch

déplacer.base_usage: Used for moving something from one place to another; se déplacer means “to get around.”

déposer.base_usage: Often means to put something down or leave it somewhere

déranger.base_usage: Used for annoying or interrupting someone; se déranger means to make the effort or inconvenience oneself

détendre.base_usage: Often reflexive: se détendre = to relax, unwind

détruire.base_usage: Used for damaging something so it no longer exists or works

effrayer.base_usage: Used with an object: someone or something causes fear

embrasser.base_usage: Most often means “to kiss”; can also mean “to hug/embrace” in some contexts

emmener.base_usage: Use for taking someone or something with you, often by vehicle

empêcher.base_usage: Often used as empêcher quelqu’un de + infinitive

enculer.base_usage: Very vulgar; often also used figuratively for "to screw over"

enfermer.base_usage: With a person or animal, it means to lock up or shut in; reflexive s'enfermer = to shut oneself in

engager.base_usage: With a direct object, often means to hire; s'engager means to commit

enlever.base_usage: Used for removing something; also for taking off clothes or parts

enregistrer.base_usage: Used most often for recording audio, video, or data

enseigner.base_usage: Used for teaching a subject or skill; often takes an object or à someone

enterrer.base_usage: Mainly used for placing a person or object in a grave or underground; also figuratively for leaving others far behind

entraîner.base_usage: Also common: with an object, it can mean ‘to lead to’ or ‘to cause’

espérer.base_usage: Used for hoping for something or that a clause happens

examiner.base_usage: Used for looking at something closely or formally, especially a person, object, or situation

exister.base_usage: Used for something being real or existing; in impersonal form, il existe can mean there is / there are

expliquer.base_usage: Used for saying why or how something is the case, or explaining that

exploser.base_usage: Used for something blowing up or bursting violently; also common in expressions like exploser de rire

exprimer.base_usage: Used for conveying feelings, ideas, or opinions

fermer.base_usage: Use for closing something; reflexive se fermer means to close up or become closed off

fiancer.base_usage: Reflexive se fiancer = to get engaged

filer.base_usage: Used for spinning yarn or thread

fonctionner.base_usage: Often used for machines, systems, or plans: “it works.”

forcer.base_usage: Used for making someone do something; also se forcer à = force oneself to

fouiller.base_usage: Most often means to search through something by hand

fournir.base_usage: Used for giving or supplying something, often with an object or to someone

foutre.base_usage: Very vulgar; also common in set phrases like se foutre de (“not care about”)

frapper.base_usage: Used for physical hitting and for knocking on a door

fumer.base_usage: Most often used for smoking cigarettes, tobacco, etc

fâcher.base_usage: Transitive: to upset/anger someone; reflexive se fâcher = to get angry

gagner.base_usage: Used for winning a game, prize, or contest

grandir.base_usage: Used for people, plants, or things becoming larger or older

gérer.base_usage: Used with a direct object: gérer quelque chose = manage or handle something

gêner.base_usage: Used for something that annoys, embarrasses, or makes things awkward

habiller.base_usage: Usually transitive: dress someone; reflexive s'habiller = to get dressed

habiter.base_usage: Used for where someone lives; often takes a place as the object

habituer.base_usage: Usually used with à: habituer quelqu’un à quelque chose

hâter.base_usage: Used for making something happen sooner or faster

identifier.base_usage: Most often used for identifying or recognizing a person, thing, or problem; also in s'identifier à for 'to identify with'

ignorer.base_usage: Often means “not know” or “be unaware that” (not English “to ignore”)

imaginer.base_usage: Used for forming a mental image or idea of something

impliquer.base_usage: Most commonly used with a direct object: “to involve.”

impressionner.base_usage: Usually used with a direct object: impressionner quelqu’un = to impress someone

informer.base_usage: Usually followed by quelqu’un de quelque chose

inquiéter.base_usage: Transitive: inquiéter quelqu’un = “to worry someone”; pronominal s’inquiéter = “to worry about.”

insister.base_usage: Often followed by sur to mean “emphasize”; also used with pour + infinitive for “insist on doing”

installer.base_usage: For equipment, apps, etc.; s'installer means to move in or settle down

interdire.base_usage: Used for forbidding an action, thing, or person; often followed by que + clause or à + person

interroger.base_usage: Used for asking someone questions, often formally or as part of an inquiry

intéresser.base_usage: Used when something interests someone: X interests Y

inventer.base_usage: Use for creating something new or coming up with an idea, device, or recipe

inviter.base_usage: Used for asking someone to come to an event or place

jeter.base_usage: Mainly used for throwing something; se jeter means “to throw oneself” or “to jump into”

juger.base_usage: Also used for opinions: juger que = “to believe/think that”; se juger = “to consider oneself”

jurer.base_usage: Used for swearing an oath or strongly affirming something; often with de + infinitive or que

lancer.base_usage: Used for throwing something; also for launching or starting something

lever.base_usage: Used for lifting or raising something; se lever means “to get up.”

libérer.base_usage: Use for freeing or releasing someone or something; reflexive se libérer means to make oneself available or get free

lier.base_usage: Use for tying or linking things together; lier à = tie/link to

lire.base_usage: Used for reading texts or information; can also be reflexive in se lire = “to be readable.”

louer.base_usage: Mainly used for renting something; also means “to praise.”

lâcher.base_usage: Commonly used for releasing something; se lâcher means to loosen up

manquer.base_usage: Often used for missing someone or something; manquer à + person means “to be missed by.”

marcher.base_usage: Most commonly means moving on foot

marier.base_usage: Also reflexive: se marier = to get married

marquer.base_usage: Used for making a mark on something; also for scoring in sports

maudire.base_usage: Used with an object: maudire quelque chose = to curse something

menacer.base_usage: Often followed by de + infinitive: menacer de faire quelque chose

mener.base_usage: Used for leading or guiding people, animals, or an action; mener à = lead to

mentir.base_usage: Usually followed by à: mentir à quelqu’un = to lie to someone

monter.base_usage: Used for moving upward; with an object, often means “to climb” or “to take up.”

naître.base_usage: Used for being born; often in the passive-like form with être

nettoyer.base_usage: Used for cleaning a place, object, or surface

nommer.base_usage: Mainly used for giving something a name; also means to appoint, and se nommer means to be called

nourrir.base_usage: Used for giving food to a person or animal

obliger.base_usage: Most often: obliger quelqu’un à + infinitive = to force someone to do something

observer.base_usage: Used for looking at something carefully

obtenir.base_usage: Used for getting or obtaining something, often in a more formal context

offrir.base_usage: Often means to give or present something to someone

opérer.base_usage: Most often used for medical surgery: a surgeon operates on a patient

ordonner.base_usage: Commonly means to give an order; also used for arranging things or ordering that something be done

organiser.base_usage: Used for arranging, planning, or setting up events, activities, or systems

paraître.base_usage: Commonly means “to seem”; for books/articles, “to come out.”

pardonner.base_usage: Commonly used with à + person and a thing being forgiven

parer.base_usage: Often in parer à + noun: to deal with or prepare for something

partager.base_usage: Use for giving others some of something or dividing something among people; se partager = divide among themselves

participer.base_usage: Usually with à + noun: “participer à” = take part in something

peindre.base_usage: Used for applying paint or making a picture with paint

permettre.base_usage: Mainly used for giving permission; permettre de + infinitive often means “to enable/allow to do”

piéger.base_usage: Used for catching an animal or person in a trap

placer.base_usage: Most often used for putting or positioning something somewhere

plaire.base_usage: Used with à + person; the French subject is what someone likes

planter.base_usage: Usually means to put plants in the ground; reflexive se planter is colloquial for “to mess up.”

pleurer.base_usage: Used for shedding tears, often because of sadness or pain

poser.base_usage: Most often used for putting something down or placing it somewhere

posséder.base_usage: Used for owning or having something

pourrir.base_usage: Used for food or organic matter becoming rotten

poursuivre.base_usage: Often transitive or reflexive: continue something / continue

pousser.base_usage: Used for physically pushing something; also in "pousser quelqu’un à + infinitive" = urge someone to do something

presser.base_usage: Commonly means to press or squeeze; se presser means to hurry

priver.base_usage: Usually takes de; se priver de means to go without or deny oneself

profiter.base_usage: Usually used as profiter de + noun: to benefit from or enjoy something

proposer.base_usage: Used for suggesting an idea or plan, or for offering something to someone

protéger.base_usage: Used for keeping someone or something safe from harm

prouver.base_usage: Used for showing that something is true or valid

provoquer.base_usage: Used for causing an event, result, or reaction

préférer.base_usage: Used for comparing choices: préférer X à Y

préparer.base_usage: Use for making something ready; reflexive se préparer means “to get ready.”

présenter.base_usage: Also used for introducing someone to someone else

prétendre.base_usage: Often used with que + clause to state something as true

prévenir.base_usage: Used for warning someone about a danger; also for informing or notifying someone

prévoir.base_usage: Often means to foresee or anticipate; prévoir de + infinitive means to plan/intend to

quitter.base_usage: Usually transitive: leave a place, person, or thing

raconter.base_usage: Used for telling a story or recounting events to someone

ramener.base_usage: Used for bringing someone or something back to a place

ranger.base_usage: Used for putting things back in order; se ranger means to settle down

rappeler.base_usage: Commonly used as rappeler qqch à qqn: to remind someone of something

rapporter.base_usage: Mainly used for bringing something back; also for reporting information

rassembler.base_usage: Used for bringing things or people together; reflexive se rassembler means “to come together.”

rater.base_usage: Commonly used for missing a bus, train, event, etc.; se rater = miss each other

rechercher.base_usage: Used for actively trying to find a person, thing, or job

recommencer.base_usage: Used for starting again after a stop, pause, or failure

reconnaître.base_usage: Mainly used for recognizing someone or something again; also for acknowledging a quality or merit

reculer.base_usage: Used for moving backward or farther away

refuser.base_usage: Commonly used with de + infinitive, or with an object to refuse or decline something

regretter.base_usage: Used for feeling sorry about something or regretting an action

rejeter.base_usage: Used for rejecting something or sending it back/away

rejoindre.base_usage: Used for joining a person, group, or place; often means meeting up with someone

relever.base_usage: Used for lifting something up; se relever means to stand up again

remarquer.base_usage: Used for spotting or noticing something, often with object or que + clause

remercier.base_usage: Used with a person or thing thanked, often with de + infinitive or pour + noun

remettre.base_usage: Commonly used for returning something to someone; also in remettre à plus tard = postpone

remonter.base_usage: Often used for moving upward again, or going back up something

remplacer.base_usage: Most commonly used for replacing a person or thing; also for taking over for someone

remplir.base_usage: Used with a direct object; se remplir means “to become full.”

renvoyer.base_usage: Most often used for sending something back

repartir.base_usage: Usually means to leave or set off again; repartir de zéro means to start again from scratch

reposer.base_usage: Commonly reflexive: se reposer = to rest

reprendre.base_usage: Used for taking something back or starting again after an interruption

représenter.base_usage: Mainly used for something that represents, depicts, or stands for something else; se représenter = to imagine

repérer.base_usage: For objects or people: to spot or locate; se repérer = to find one's way

respecter.base_usage: Used for people, rules, wishes, etc.; often means “respect” or “follow/obey” a rule

respirer.base_usage: Used for breathing air; with an object, it can mean to breathe in or inhale

ressembler.base_usage: Usually ressembler à + noun: to look like someone or something

ressentir.base_usage: Usually followed by an object: feel an emotion, sensation, or effect

restaurer.base_usage: Mainly used for repairing or renovating something; se restaurer means to eat or refresh oneself

retenir.base_usage: Often means to keep back or hold back; se retenir de + infinitive = to refrain from

retirer.base_usage: Used for taking something off or away; also for withdrawing money

retourner.base_usage: Often used for returning something to someone; se retourner means “to turn around.”

risquer.base_usage: Commonly means “to risk”; with de + infinitive, it can mean “to be likely to.”

rouler.base_usage: Used for turning over or moving on wheels

ruiner.base_usage: Used for destroying, spoiling, or financially ruining something/someone; se ruiner = to go broke

réaliser.base_usage: Commonly means to achieve/accomplish; also used in "réaliser que" = "to realize that"

récupérer.base_usage: Used for getting something back; also for picking someone up

réfléchir.base_usage: Often used with à: to think about something or consider doing something

régler.base_usage: With an object, it means to adjust or set something; se régler is the reflexive form

réparer.base_usage: Used for fixing something; se réparer means to heal or recover

répondre.base_usage: Usually followed by à: répondre à quelqu’un / quelque chose

répéter.base_usage: Used for saying or doing something again; often with an object

réserver.base_usage: Used for booking places, tables, tickets, etc.; réserver à quelqu’un means to keep something for someone

résoudre.base_usage: Most often used for solving a problem or issue; also with de + infinitive: ‘decide to’

réussir.base_usage: Often used as réussir à + infinitive = to manage to do

réveiller.base_usage: Usually transitive: wake someone up; reflexive se réveiller means “to wake up.”

révéler.base_usage: Used for making something known; se révéler means “to turn out to be.”

s'ennuyer.base_usage: Reflexive verb: used for feeling bored, often with an activity or situation

sacrer.base_usage: Used for crowning a ruler or winner; also for religious consecration

saisir.base_usage: Most often: to grab or seize something quickly

sauter.base_usage: Also used with an object: "to jump over" something

se dépêcher.base_usage: Reflexive; often used as se dépêcher de + infinitive: hurry to do something

se fâcher.base_usage: Reflexive: usually means becoming angry, often with contre + person

se moquer.base_usage: Reflexive; se moquer de someone/something means to make fun of them, or to not care about them

se tromper.base_usage: Reflexive; often se tromper de + noun = get the wrong one or mistake one thing for another

signer.base_usage: Use for putting your signature on a document

signifier.base_usage: Most often used for “to mean” or “to indicate”; can also mean formally notify someone

soigner.base_usage: Use for treating someone medically or caring for someone/something; reflexive se soigner means to get treatment or take care of oneself

sonner.base_usage: Used for bells, alarms, phones, or sounds

souffrir.base_usage: souffrir de + noun = suffer from something

souhaiter.base_usage: Used for expressing wishes; also in "souhaiter que" = "to hope that"

sourire.base_usage: Often used with à + person: “smile at someone.”

soutenir.base_usage: Used for supporting a person, cause, or idea; in formal French it can also mean “to claim”

subir.base_usage: Used for experiencing something unpleasant, often a change or hardship

supporter.base_usage: Most often in negatives: ne pas supporter + noun/infinitive = can't stand

supposer.base_usage: Often used with que: “supposer que” = to assume/guess that

surveiller.base_usage: Use for watching someone or something closely, often to supervise or prevent problems

survivre.base_usage: Usually with à: survive something or someone, often meaning “outlive.”

séparer.base_usage: Use for putting things apart; se séparer means to split up or break up

taire.base_usage: Often reflexive: se taire = to be silent

tenter.base_usage: Often means to try or attempt something; with a person, it can mean to tempt

terminer.base_usage: Most often with a direct object: “to finish something.”

toucher.base_usage: Basic physical contact; se toucher = to be in contact

tourner.base_usage: Used for turning something or changing direction

traiter.base_usage: Often means dealing with a problem or issue; traiter de + noun means to call someone something

transformer.base_usage: Used for changing something into a different form or state

traverser.base_usage: Used for moving across a street, place, or area

traîner.base_usage: Used for dragging something; also for hanging around or moving slowly, especially se traîner

tromper.base_usage: Mainly used for deceiving someone; se tromper means to make a mistake or be wrong

témoigner.base_usage: Used mainly for speaking as a witness or providing evidence

valoir.base_usage: Used for value or worth: valoir + noun = “to be worth” or “to equal in value.”

vendre.base_usage: Use for selling goods or belongings, often with a buyer marked by à

violer.base_usage: Used for breaking a law, rule, or sacred place

virer.base_usage: Commonly used informally for firing someone; also for bank transfers and turning/veering

voler.base_usage: Mainly for movement through the air; also common: to steal

voyager.base_usage: Used for going from place to place, especially over a distance

vérifier.base_usage: Usually used for checking something; reflexive se vérifier means “to prove true.”

échapper.base_usage: Commonly in échapper à = to escape from, elude

échouer.base_usage: Often used with à + infinitive: fail to do something

éclater.base_usage: Most often used for something breaking open or exploding; also for laughter or emotions bursting out

écouter.base_usage: Use for paying attention to sounds or to what someone says

écraser.base_usage: Used for physically crushing or flattening something; also for overpowering an opponent

écrire.base_usage: Used for producing written text or content

élever.base_usage: Used for raising children, animals, or things upward

éliminer.base_usage: Used for removing something or getting rid of an error, problem, or opponent

éloigner.base_usage: Often used reflexively: s'éloigner = to move away / walk away

énerver.base_usage: Commonly used with an object: something annoys or irritates someone

épouser.base_usage: Used with a person: to marry; also figuratively, to adopt or embrace an idea

établir.base_usage: Used for founding something or making it official

étudier.base_usage: Used for studying at school or university, or studying a subject carefully

éviter.base_usage: Used for avoiding a person, thing, or situation

abattre.base_usage: Used for bringing something down, especially a tree, wall, or other object

abrutir.base_usage: Used for something that makes a person or their mind less sharp or lively

accomplir.base_usage: Used for completing a task, mission, or goal

accrocher.base_usage: Most often transitive: to hang or attach something somewhere; reflexive s'accrocher à means to cling to

accueillir.base_usage: Use for welcoming guests, visitors, or an event

accéder.base_usage: Usually used with à + noun: accéder à un fichier, à un site, etc

accélérer.base_usage: Used for making something faster or for going faster; often with à before a speed

achever.base_usage: Used for completing an action or making something finished

acquérir.base_usage: Used for acquiring something new, often in a formal or written context

activer.base_usage: Often used for devices, alarms, or functions: “to turn on” / “to enable”

adapter.base_usage: Used for changing something to suit a new purpose or situation

adopter.base_usage: Used for adopting a child, animal, idea, or policy

adresser.base_usage: Used for directing words, letters, or actions to someone

affamer.base_usage: Usually transitive: to cause someone or a group to be hungry or starve

affirmer.base_usage: Used for saying something firmly or confidently

agresser.base_usage: Used for attacking or assaulting a person

allonger.base_usage: Used for making something longer or extending it

améliorer.base_usage: Used with a direct object: to make something better

analyser.base_usage: Used for examining something in detail, especially data, text, or a problem

appliquer.base_usage: Used for putting a substance on something; reflexive s'appliquer à means “to apply to.”

approcher.base_usage: Transitive: bring something closer; reflexive s'approcher de means come closer to

approprier.base_usage: Often means to adapt something to a person, purpose, or context; reflexive s’approprier means to take for oneself

approuver.base_usage: Used for agreeing with, or officially approving, something

appuyer.base_usage: Commonly used for pressing a button or surface; reflexive s'appuyer sur means to lean on or rely on

arracher.base_usage: Used for forcefully pulling something out or away from something/someone

augmenter.base_usage: Used for making something larger or higher; also intransitive for amounts or prices

autopsier.base_usage: Used for a doctor or pathologist examining a body to find the cause of death

avertir.base_usage: Usually means to warn someone about a danger or problem

balancer.base_usage: Most often: to swing or rock back and forth

baser.base_usage: Usually in baser quelque chose sur quelque chose = base something on something

bloquer.base_usage: Used for stopping movement or access, or blocking someone online

botter.base_usage: Used for hitting something with the foot, often in sports

boucher.base_usage: Used for blocking or plugging a hole, pipe, road, etc

boucler.base_usage: Also common informally for finishing or wrapping up something; se boucler = to curl

bouger.base_usage: Used for physical movement; can also be transitive: bouger quelque chose = move something

bouleverser.base_usage: Most often used for strongly upsetting someone or deeply affecting a situation

buter.translation: to bump into; to trip over
buter.base_usage: Often used with sur/contre for accidentally hitting or stumbling over something

capturer.base_usage: Used for catching and taking control of a person, animal, or thing

choper.base_usage: Colloquial: used for catching something or grabbing it quickly

choquer.base_usage: Used for surprising or upsetting someone strongly

cingler.base_usage: Usually for wind, rain, or a whip hitting hard

circuler.base_usage: Used for things, people, air, news, or traffic moving around

citer.base_usage: Used for quoting someone or citing examples, sources, or words

classer.base_usage: Used for putting things into order or categories

cogner.base_usage: Often used for striking something; se cogner = to bump into something

coller.base_usage: Use for putting something onto a surface with glue or pressure

communiquer.base_usage: Often used with an object or à + person: to convey or inform

comparer.base_usage: Used for comparing things or likening someone/something to another

comporter.base_usage: Transitive; often used for parts, features, or risks: “comporter + object”

composer.base_usage: Usually for music, writing, or making a work; se composer de = consist of

compromettre.base_usage: Usually used with an object: to put something at risk; reflexive se compromettre = to risk one's reputation

concevoir.base_usage: Commonly means to design or plan something; also used for abstract ideas

condamner.base_usage: Often used in law: to sentence someone to a penalty

connecter.base_usage: Usually means to connect or link something; reflexive se connecter means to log in

consacrer.base_usage: Often used with à: devote something to someone/something; reflexive se consacrer à = devote oneself to

consulter.base_usage: Used for consulting a person, source, or expert; se consulter = consult each other

contenter.base_usage: Often used with a direct object; se contenter de means “to be content with” or “to settle for.”

contrarier.base_usage: Used for making someone feel upset or annoyed, or for thwarting plans

convenir.base_usage: Most often used for something that suits someone: ça me convient

coter.base_usage: Used for stating a price or market value; also in technical drawing, to dimension a plan

cracher.base_usage: Usually used for expelling saliva, food, or something from the mouth

creuser.base_usage: Used for making a hole or removing earth/material

crever.base_usage: Commonly used for tires, balloons, or other things breaking open; se crever means to work oneself to exhaustion

crier.base_usage: Used for speaking very loudly, often to call out or express strong emotion

croiser.base_usage: Most often used for meeting or passing someone by chance; se croiser = to pass each other

céder.base_usage: With à, it means to yield or give in; se céder means to be transferred

célébrer.base_usage: Used for marking an event or person; se célébrer = be celebrated

dessiner.base_usage: Use for making a picture or design with a pencil, pen, etc.; se dessiner means “to take shape.”

destiner.base_usage: Usually with à: destiner quelque chose à quelqu’un = intend it for someone

disposer.base_usage: Also in disposer de + noun: to have, have at one's disposal

disputer.base_usage: Often used for a match or competition; se disputer means “to quarrel.”

divorcer.base_usage: Used for ending a marriage; often intransitive: 'to get divorced'

dormir.base_usage: Used for the basic act or state of sleeping

douter.base_usage: Used with de + noun/pronoun, or que + clause, to express doubt

draguer.base_usage: Commonly used as draguer + person: to flirt with or chat up someone

droguer.base_usage: Often reflexive: se droguer = to use drugs

débarquer.base_usage: Used for leaving a boat or plane; also colloquially “to arrive.”

déborder.base_usage: Often used with de: “déborder de” = to be overflowing with something

débrouiller.base_usage: Also in se débrouiller: to manage, cope, or get by

débuter.base_usage: Used for starting an activity, event, or career

décevoir.base_usage: Usually takes a direct object: décevoir quelqu’un

déchirer.base_usage: Used for tearing or ripping something into pieces

décoller.base_usage: For planes, birds, etc.; se décoller = come unstuck

déconner.base_usage: Colloquial: used when a machine or system malfunctions

décrire.base_usage: Used for saying what someone or something is like; often with à + person

décrocher.base_usage: Also used for answering the phone; reflexive se décrocher means to come loose

décéder.base_usage: Formal verb for dying, especially in official or news contexts

défier.base_usage: Often in défier quelqu'un de + infinitive: dare someone to do something; reflexive se défier de means to distrust or be wary of

défiler.base_usage: Used for troops or people moving past in a parade or procession; reflexive se défiler = to back out

défoncer.base_usage: Often means to smash or wreck something by force

dégager.base_usage: Common for freeing or clearing something; also used for smells, heat, etc. 'se dégager' = to become clear

dégoûter.base_usage: Usually means that something disgusts or repels someone

déguiser.base_usage: Often reflexive: se déguiser = to dress up in costume

démarrer.base_usage: Commonly used for starting a vehicle, engine, or process

démissionner.base_usage: Usually for leaving a job or office voluntarily

démolir.base_usage: Usually used for tearing down or destroying a building or structure

déménager.base_usage: Usually means changing homes; can also take a direct object for moving things

dépenser.base_usage: Usually means spending money, or time/effort

dépêcher.base_usage: Most often means to send someone or something quickly; se dépêcher means to hurry

désespérer.base_usage: Often used with de + infinitive: to lose hope of doing something

désirer.base_usage: Formal; used for wanting something or wishing that something happens

détenir.base_usage: Mainly: to have or hold something in one’s possession; also used for legal detention

déterminer.base_usage: Most often: to establish or figure out something by investigation or reasoning

détester.base_usage: Used for strong dislike; often with a direct object

développer.base_usage: Used for making something more developed; also se développer = to grow or expand

effacer.base_usage: Used for removing writing, marks, data, or other traces

effectuer.base_usage: Used for doing a task, action, or procedure, often in formal contexts

effondrer.base_usage: Often used reflexively: se effondrer = to collapse or cave in

emballer.base_usage: Used for wrapping or packing an object, especially a gift

embarquer.base_usage: Used for getting onto a ship, plane, or vehicle; can also mean to load something onto it

embaucher.base_usage: Used for hiring a person for a job

embêter.base_usage: Used for annoying or pestering someone

alternate_glosses: to move into a new home
emménager.base_usage: Used for moving into a new home or place

employer.base_usage: Commonly means “to use” something; also “to hire/employ” a person

empoisonner.base_usage: Used for putting poison into something; reflexive s’empoisonner means “to poison oneself.”

emporter.base_usage: Used for taking something away from a place, often with you

enchanter.base_usage: Usually means to delight or please someone

encourager.base_usage: Used for giving someone support or motivation

endormir.base_usage: Usually transitive: endormir quelqu'un = put someone to sleep; reflexive se endormir = fall asleep

enfoncer.base_usage: Used for forcing a nail or similar object into something

enfuir.base_usage: Usually used as the reflexive s'enfuir: to run away or flee from a place

ennuyer.base_usage: Usually transitive: “to bore someone”; also “to annoy/bother.”

enquêter.base_usage: Usually used with sur: enquêter sur quelque chose

entourer.base_usage: Used for physically or figuratively surrounding something

entretenir.base_usage: Used for keeping something in good condition or sustaining it; also for supporting a family

envisager.base_usage: Used for considering a possibility or plan; often with a noun or de + infinitive

espionner.base_usage: Used for secretly watching or monitoring someone/something

exciter.base_usage: Often means to arouse interest, emotion, or desire

exiger.base_usage: Used when asking for something as necessary or non-negotiable; often with que + clause or de + person

exposer.base_usage: Commonly used for showing artworks or other objects publicly

exécuter.base_usage: Used for carrying out a plan, order, or task

fabriquer.base_usage: Used for making things, especially objects; se fabriquer can mean “to make for oneself.”

fasciner.base_usage: Usually means to attract and hold someone’s attention strongly

figurer.base_usage: Often in figurer sur + noun: “to appear” or “to be listed.”

filmer.base_usage: Used for recording something with a camera; also in se filmer, “to film oneself.”

fixer.base_usage: Used for setting or establishing something; se fixer can mean to settle down

flipper.base_usage: Colloquial: to panic or worry a lot, often with de + infinitive

foncer.base_usage: Used for moving forward very fast, often straight at something

fonder.base_usage: Used for creating or establishing an organization; also in fonder sur = to base on

fondre.base_usage: Mainly for something turning liquid from heat; also se fondre = to blend in

former.base_usage: Commonly used for training or educating people

fréquenter.base_usage: Often used for a place you go to regularly; se fréquenter means to see each other often

fuir.base_usage: Often takes a direct object: fuir quelque chose = flee or avoid something

fuser.base_usage: Usually intransitive: questions, comments, or rumors come out quickly and in great number

fêter.base_usage: Used for celebrating an event or occasion; also se fêter = be celebrated

garer.base_usage: Used for parking a vehicle; reflexive se garer = to park

glacer.base_usage: With an object, it usually means to ice or glaze something; se glacer means to freeze over

glisser.base_usage: Often used for moving something smoothly; se glisser means to sneak in

gonfler.base_usage: Also reflexive: se gonfler = to swell up

grever.base_usage: Usually of taxes, costs, or debt burdening a budget or situation

grimper.base_usage: Also used for prices, temperatures, etc. going up

guider.base_usage: Used for showing the way or leading a person or group

guérir.base_usage: Often transitive: cure an illness or heal someone; intransitive: recover

gâcher.base_usage: Used for ruining something, or wasting time/money/effort

harper.base_usage: Used for catching or grabbing something suddenly, often with a hook or claws

humilier.base_usage: Used for making someone feel ashamed or inferior, especially in public

hurler.base_usage: Used for loud shouting or crying out, often from anger, fear, pain, or laughter

hésiter.base_usage: Often used with à + infinitive: hesitate to do something

imposer.base_usage: Often with à: to impose something on someone; also used for rules or requirements

inclure.base_usage: Used for putting something as part of a larger group or list

indiquer.base_usage: Often means to show or point out something; with à + person, to tell someone

infecter.base_usage: Used for a disease, germ, or harmful thing spreading into a person, animal, or system

infiltrer.base_usage: Usually transitive; reflexive s'infiltrer means “to seep / slip into.”

inscrire.base_usage: Used for putting a name on a list or enrolling someone in a school/activity

inspirer.base_usage: Used for causing an idea, feeling, or reaction; s'inspirer de means “to draw inspiration from.”

insulter.base_usage: Use with a direct object; s'insulter means to insult each other

interrompre.base_usage: Used for stopping a person, action, or event suddenly

intervenir.base_usage: Used when someone steps in to help, stop, or affect a situation

justifier.base_usage: Used for showing that an action, decision, or statement is reasonable; se justifier = to defend oneself

larguer.base_usage: Used for letting go of something; slangily, to dump someone

laurer.base_usage: Usually literary or formal: to honor someone with laurels or laurel leaves

livrer.base_usage: Used for delivering or handing something to someone; often with à

localiser.base_usage: Use with an object or person to find or determine where they are

lutter.base_usage: Often used with contre or pour: to fight against / fight for

maintenir.base_usage: Used for keeping something in a state or position; se maintenir = to remain/stay

manager.base_usage: Used with an object: to manage a team, project, or business

manipuler.base_usage: Often means to handle or operate an object; also used for influencing someone unfairly

mentionner.base_usage: Use for briefly referring to someone or something

merder.base_usage: Often intransitive: ça merde = things go wrong

modifier.base_usage: Used for changing or adjusting something, often slightly or formally

moquer.base_usage: Most often in se moquer de + noun: to make fun of

mordre.base_usage: Used for biting people or things; se mordre les doigts de + infinitive means to regret doing something

muser.base_usage: Used for moving or spending time aimlessly, often without doing anything useful

mélanger.base_usage: Used for combining ingredients, substances, or colors

mêler.base_usage: Usually means to mix; also mêler quelqu’un à quelque chose = involve someone in something

nager.base_usage: Use for moving through water by swimming; it can take a distance as an object

navrer.base_usage: Often formal: used for causing deep sadness; in être navré de, it means “to be sorry.”

noter.base_usage: Often means to write down or make a note of something

noyer.base_usage: Often transitive; se noyer means “to drown” oneself

négocier.base_usage: Used for discussing and agreeing terms, prices, or deals

obséder.base_usage: Usually transitive: something obsesses or haunts someone

offenser.base_usage: Usually means to hurt or insult someone

opposer.base_usage: Used for putting one thing against another, or for formally opposing someone/something

oser.base_usage: Used for having the courage to do something, often before an infinitive

palmer.base_usage: Used mainly in card magic: to hide something in the hand

paniquer.base_usage: Used for becoming suddenly very anxious or frightened; often in phrases like paniquer à l’idée de + infinitive

parcourir.base_usage: With an object, it means to go through a place or quickly read/scan something

parier.base_usage: Used for wagering or for saying confidently that something will happen

parvenir.base_usage: Usually with à + infinitive: manage to do something

pendre.base_usage: Used for something hanging or dangling; also in reflexive form, se pendre, “to hang oneself”

peser.base_usage: Used for measuring the weight of something; reflexive se peser means “to weigh oneself.”

piger.base_usage: Colloquial; used for understanding an idea or explanation

piquer.base_usage: Most often used for insects or sharp objects: to sting or prick

pisser.base_usage: Informal/vulgar: used for urinating, e.g. pisser sur + noun

plaider.base_usage: Often used in court; plaider que means “to argue that.”

plaindre.base_usage: Transitive: used to express pity for someone or something

planifier.base_usage: Used for deciding or arranging something in advance

planquer.base_usage: Colloquial: hide something or, with se, hide oneself

plonger.base_usage: Also used transitively: to dip or immerse something

poignarder.base_usage: Use for stabbing someone or something with a knife or similar weapon

pointer.base_usage: Used for directing attention with a finger, gesture, or sign

polir.base_usage: Used for making a surface shiny or smoother; reflexive se polir = become polished

pratiquer.base_usage: Used for doing an activity regularly, especially a sport, hobby, or discipline

prier.base_usage: Used mainly for praying to God or a saint

procéder.base_usage: Usually formal: procéder à + noun means to carry out or undertake something

produire.base_usage: Most common for making or generating goods, results, or effects; reflexive se produire means “to occur” or “to perform.”

promener.translation: to walk; to take for a walk
promener.base_usage: Usually transitive: promener un chien = to walk a dog; se promener = to take a walk

promettre.base_usage: Used for making a promise or commitment

prononcer.base_usage: Also in se prononcer: to express an opinion

prostituer.base_usage: Transitive use often means to exploit or debase something for gain; reflexive se prostituer means to prostitute oneself

provenir.base_usage: Usually in provenir de: something comes from a source, place, or cause

préciser.base_usage: Used for making something more exact or clear, often with an object or a clause

pécher.base_usage: Mainly religious or moral: pécher par + noun can mean “to err because of …”

pénétrer.base_usage: Used for going into or through something, literally or figuratively

péter.base_usage: Literal for passing gas; also in informal phrases like péter quelque chose, meaning “to break.”

ramasser.base_usage: Used for picking things up from the ground or gathering them together

raser.base_usage: Often reflexive: se raser = to shave oneself; also used for razing buildings

rassurer.base_usage: Used when you make someone less worried or afraid

rattraper.base_usage: Commonly used for reaching someone or something again; reflexive se rattraper can mean to make amends

recommander.base_usage: Used for suggesting or advising something to someone

redevenir.base_usage: Used with a noun or adjective: become something again

relier.base_usage: Used for joining things together or making a connection

relâcher.base_usage: Most often used for letting someone or something go free

rembourser.base_usage: Used for paying back money or reimbursing someone

remporter.base_usage: Used with a direct object: win a match, prize, or victory

renseigner.base_usage: Used for informing someone; se renseigner means to inquire

renverser.base_usage: Commonly used for causing something to tip over or spill; also for overthrowing a government

reparler.base_usage: Usually with à/de: talk again to someone or about something

repasser.base_usage: For clothes and fabrics: to iron

reporter.base_usage: Used for moving an event or action to a later time

repousser.base_usage: Used for physically pushing something back or driving someone/something away

ressortir.base_usage: Often used when something becomes more visible or prominent

retarder.base_usage: Used for making something happen later than planned

rigoler.base_usage: Informal: often means to laugh or joke around

rire.base_usage: Commonly used for laughing; with de, it means “to laugh at” someone or something

rompre.base_usage: Commonly used for breaking something; also in se rompre, “to break / snap.”

roser.base_usage: Used for giving something a pink or rosy color, often in descriptive or literary language

rouer.base_usage: Most often in rouer de coups: to beat someone severely

réagir.base_usage: Most often used as réagir à quelque chose: to react/respond to something

réduire.base_usage: Used for making something smaller, less, or lower

régner.base_usage: Used for a ruler’s reign, or for something to prevail in a place or situation

réunir.base_usage: Reflexive se réunir means to meet up or get together

rêver.base_usage: Used for sleeping dreams and for hoping or imagining something

sacrifier.base_usage: Used for giving up something; also reflexive: se sacrifier = to sacrifice oneself

saigner.base_usage: Used for blood loss; also transitive: saigner un poisson = to bleed a fish

satisfaire.base_usage: Used with a direct object: satisfaire une demande, un besoin, des critères

secouer.base_usage: Used for shaking something; se secouer means to get moving or snap out of it

souffler.base_usage: Used for blowing air; also in expressions like souffler les bougies

soulager.base_usage: Used for easing pain, discomfort, or a burden; reflexive se soulager means “to relieve oneself.”

suggérer.base_usage: Use for putting an idea forward or implying something indirectly

suicider.base_usage: Usually reflexive: se suicider = to take one's own life

transmettre.base_usage: Often used for information, signals, or diseases; se transmettre = be transmitted

venger.base_usage: Used with a person or wrong: to retaliate for it

verser.base_usage: Most often: to pour a liquid; also used for paying money

épargner.base_usage: Most often means to put money aside; also used for sparing someone or something

épuiser.base_usage: Often used for using up resources, options, or strength

éteindre.base_usage: Used for lights, devices, fires, etc

étendre.base_usage: Used for putting something out over a surface or area

étonner.base_usage: Used with a direct object: to surprise someone

apeurer.base_usage: Used for causing fear in someone

induire.base_usage: Formal: usually means to cause or bring about

abréger.base_usage: Often means to make something shorter in length or duration

émoucheter.base_usage: Usually said of a blade, point, or edge; rare in modern use

émoudre.base_usage: Used for sharpening a blade or tool on a stone

émousser.base_usage: Used for making an edge less sharp; also figuratively, to weaken or reduce intensity

émoustiller.base_usage: Usually means to mildly arouse or excite someone; often a little literary

émouvoir.base_usage: Used when something causes strong feelings, often sympathy or sadness

émuler.base_usage: Formal; often used for imitating a model, or in computing for making one system behave like another

émulsifier.base_usage: Used for making two liquids mix into a stable emulsion

émulsionner.base_usage: Used in cooking, chemistry, and cosmetics for mixing liquids into a stable emulsion

émécher.base_usage: Usually means to make someone a little drunk; also used reflexively for becoming tipsy

énamourer.base_usage: Usually reflexive: s'énamourer de = to fall in love with

énieller.base_usage: Agricultural term for removing nielle or cockleweed from grain

énoncer.base_usage: Use for formally stating or setting out an idea, rule, or position

énouer.base_usage: Rare; usually means to tie something into a knot or loop

énucléer.base_usage: Used in medicine for surgically removing an eye or a small mass

énumérer.base_usage: Used in formal writing for naming items one by one

épaissir.base_usage: Used for a liquid or mixture becoming thicker, or making it thicker; also in the reflexive form s’épaissir

épaler.base_usage: Nautical: to position something so it lies on the beam

épamprer.base_usage: Used in viticulture for removing unwanted vine shoots

épancher.base_usage: Mainly for liquids or medical fluid buildup; reflexive s'épancher means 'to pour one's heart out'

épandre.base_usage: Mainly used for spreading a substance over the ground or a surface, especially in farming

épanneler.base_usage: Especially in sculpture or architecture, to remove excess material and give something its rough shape

épanner.base_usage: Used in stoneworking for shaping a block roughly

épanouir.base_usage: Often reflexive: s’épanouir means to flourish or thrive, especially for a person, talent, or plant

éparpiller.base_usage: Used for sending small things or people in different directions

épater.base_usage: Used for surprising or impressing someone strongly

épaufrer.base_usage: Used for a surface or edge losing small flakes, especially stone or concrete

épauler.base_usage: Often means to give practical help or backing to someone

épeler.base_usage: Used for naming the letters of a word one by one

éperonner.base_usage: Mainly used for urging a horse forward with spurs; also figuratively, to encourage action

épeuler.base_usage: For a bottle, glass, or pot: to chip the shoulder or rim

épeurer.base_usage: To make someone afraid; current but uncommon, often regional

épicer.base_usage: Usually in cooking: to add spices to food

épier.base_usage: Usually means watching someone secretly or closely

épierrer.base_usage: Used for removing stones from soil or a field

épiler.base_usage: Usually for removing body or facial hair, especially by waxing or plucking

épiloguer.base_usage: Often with sur: to argue or talk over something in a finicky, pointless way

épinceler.base_usage: Rare; mainly for securing fabric or paper with pins

épincer.base_usage: Used in gardening for removing the tip of a plant to make it branch

épinceter.base_usage: Used for removing small bits or hairs with tweezers or pincers

épiner.base_usage: Used for taking thorns, spines, or similar sharp bits off something

épingler.base_usage: Used for fixing something in place with a pin or similar fastener

épisser.base_usage: Used mainly for ropes, cables, or fibers: to join them by overlapping and twisting them

éployer.base_usage: Literary or old-fashioned: to unfold or spread out something

éplucher.base_usage: Used for removing the skin or outer layer, especially of fruit or vegetables

épointer.base_usage: Used for removing or dulling a point, tip, or edge

éponger.base_usage: Used for removing liquid with a sponge or cloth

épontiller.base_usage: Used in technical contexts for supporting something with a prop or stanchion

épouiller.base_usage: Used mainly for removing lice from a person or animal

époumoner.base_usage: Often used in the reflexive form s'époumoner: to shout until your voice gets hoarse

épousseter.base_usage: Used for removing dust from furniture, shelves, or other surfaces

époustoufler.base_usage: Used for something that surprises or impresses very strongly

époutier.base_usage: Old or regional verb for rolling or wallowing about, often in dirt or mud

époutir.base_usage: Usually means to remove dust from something

épouvanter.base_usage: Used for causing strong fear; often in a literary or formal tone

éprendre.base_usage: Usually used reflexively: s’éprendre de + person/thing

éprouver.base_usage: Often used for experiencing feelings, hardships, or emotions; also for testing something

épucer.base_usage: Used for taking fleas off an animal or person

épurer.base_usage: Often used for removing impurities or simplifying a style, process, or system

épépiner.base_usage: Used for removing seeds from fruit or vegetables, especially in cooking

alternate_glosses: to dress into a square shape
équarrir.base_usage: Used for shaping wood, stone, or a carcass into a roughly square form

équerrer.base_usage: Used in carpentry or geometry for making something square

équeuter.base_usage: Used for removing stems, tails, or tops from fruit or vegetables

équilibrer.base_usage: Used for making something balanced or evenly distributed

équiper.base_usage: Used for supplying someone or something with needed gear or tools

équivaloir.base_usage: Usually with à: to mean “be equivalent to” or “add up to.”

équivoquer.base_usage: Used for speaking in a deliberately ambiguous way to avoid a clear answer

éradiquer.base_usage: Used for completely removing a disease, problem, or group

érafler.base_usage: Used for a light scratch or scrape on skin or a surface

érailler.base_usage: Usually of fabric or edges: to make slightly ragged; of a voice, to become hoarse

érayer.base_usage: Used for making light scratches or lines on a surface

éreinter.base_usage: Mainly used for making someone very tired; reflexive s’éreinter = work oneself to exhaustion

ériger.base_usage: Often used for formally raising or building something, especially a monument or institution

éroder.base_usage: Used for something being gradually worn away or weakened, literally or figuratively

érotiser.base_usage: Used for making something more sexual or erotic, often in art, media, or language

éructer.base_usage: Formal word for belching; everyday French usually says roter

établer.base_usage: Used for putting animals in a stable

étager.base_usage: Usually means to place things in layers or tiers; often reflexive for things spread out in levels

étalager.base_usage: Rare; used for putting goods or objects out on display

étaler.base_usage: Used for putting things flat or in an arranged way

étalinguer.base_usage: Nautical: to attach a cable or anchor chain firmly to the anchor or fitting

étaliser.base_usage: Used for placing or spreading something out evenly; rare and context-dependent

étalonner.base_usage: For instruments and devices: to adjust or check against a standard

étamer.base_usage: Used for coating a metal surface with tin

étamper.base_usage: Used mainly for punching or stamping material with a die

étancher.base_usage: Often used with thirst or a desire: étancher sa soif

étançonner.base_usage: Used for supporting something with a strut or brace, especially in construction

étarquer.base_usage: Used especially in sailing for tightening a sail or line

étatiser.base_usage: Used for putting a business, industry, or service under state control

étayer.base_usage: Often used for supporting a claim with evidence; also for physically shoring something up

éterniser.base_usage: Often used in the reflexive: s’éterniser = to drag on, last too long

éternuer.base_usage: For the sudden involuntary act of sneezing

éthérifier.base_usage: Used in chemistry for converting a compound into an ether or adding an ether group

éthériser.base_usage: Used for inducing anesthesia with ether, now mainly in historical or specialized contexts

étinceler.base_usage: Used for light or surfaces that flash or shine in small bright points

étioler.base_usage: Usually of plants: to weaken and pale them through lack of light

étiqueter.base_usage: Use for putting a label on something, or marking it with a category

étirer.base_usage: To make something longer by pulling it; also for muscles or the body

étoffer.base_usage: Used for making a plan, text, or offer more complete or detailed

étoiler.base_usage: Used for making something covered or marked with star shapes; sometimes for a star-like crack or pattern

étouffer.base_usage: Used for blocking breathing; also for smothering or stifling something

étouper.base_usage: Used for blocking or packing a gap, hole, or opening

étoupiller.base_usage: Used for filling or sealing something with tow/oakum

étourdir.base_usage: Often means to make someone dizzy or briefly unable to think clearly

étreindre.base_usage: Usually means to hold someone or something tightly

étrenner.translation: to use for the first time
alternate_glosses: to wear for the first time
étrenner.base_usage: Most often for wearing or using something new for the first time

étriller.base_usage: Literally, to groom a horse with a currycomb; also used figuratively for beating someone badly

étriper.base_usage: Mainly used for opening a body or animal and removing the insides

étriquer.base_usage: Used for making something narrower, tighter, or more cramped

étronçonner.base_usage: Rare; used for cutting something into short sections, often with a tool

étrécir.base_usage: Often transitive; also reflexive: s’étrécir = to get narrower

étrésillonner.base_usage: Used in construction for supporting something with temporary struts or braces

étuver.base_usage: Used in cooking for gently cooking food in a covered pan with little liquid

étêter.base_usage: Used for removing the head or the top part of something, especially a plant or object

évader.base_usage: Mainly in the reflexive form s'évader: to escape from a place, prison, or confinement

évaluer.base_usage: Used for judging the value, quality, or level of something

évangéliser.base_usage: Mainly used for spreading Christianity; also figuratively for promoting an idea or cause

évanouir.base_usage: Usually reflexive: s'évanouir = to faint; also used figuratively for things that fade or disappear

évaporer.base_usage: Often used in science/chemistry for a liquid changing into vapor; also in the pronominal form s’évaporer

évaser.base_usage: To make something broader at one end, especially an opening or garment

éveiller.base_usage: Used for waking someone or stirring a feeling, interest, or awareness

éventer.base_usage: Used for airing something, or figuratively for revealing a secret or plan

éventrer.base_usage: Usually for violently opening a person, animal, or object by force

évertuer.base_usage: Usually pronominal: s'évertuer à + infinitive means to keep trying hard

évider.base_usage: Used for removing the inside of something, leaving a hollow shell

évincer.base_usage: Used for removing someone from a job, group, or contest

évoluer.base_usage: Used for change over time, or for a person/situation to develop

évoquer.base_usage: Used for bringing a memory, feeling, or image to mind; also for briefly mentioning something in formal speech or writing

ôter.base_usage: Used for taking something away or off; often a bit formal or written
